kept.text:
You're missing the key difference @roundrobin42. Kalshi profiting through fees doesn't mean they are agnostic to how their users do; it means they make more money the slower their users lose.

The slower users lose, the more money Kalshi makes relative to market makers from the user, because more money is churned and they collect more in fees. It's pretty simple.

Let's say a user has $100 to "predict" with. Assume a 3% avg take fee. If their ROI pre-take fee is -7% (-10% after fee) it takes $1,000 in volume to lose their $100. Kalshi collects $30 in fees. The other $70 goes to market makers.

If the user has a 0% ROI (-3% after take fee), it takes $3,333 in volume to lose the $100, and Kalshi generates $100 in fees from the user. In this case, the market maker is breaking even and Kalshi is capturing all of the profit.

Kalshi needs market makers to function, but in their ideal world MMs make just enough money to stay, and not a penny more. That's exactly what rebate structures are designed to calibrate.

kept.text:
How does the stock market keep going up while the economy is one of the worst for young people in modern history?

Because we are the ones who paid for it. The bank bailouts, COVID era stimulus, and cheap debt were paid for with the silent tax of inflation.

Older generations willingly sold out future generations to extract wealth. That’s how the fiat system works.

Money is lent into existence, debasing the current money in the system. Before you were even born, you were in debt.

Instead of a monthly payment, they simply decided to never give you the chance at any economic stability.

And people wonder why Gen Z wants to see the economy burn. We are tired of being the boomers’ exit liquidity.
